header.stance: Treasury relief held into the NY close, but energy risk left the long end resistant
header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 25 close extended the Treasury rally, but its composition changed from Wednesday’s belly-led bull flattening to a modest bull steepening. The two-year yield closed at 4.09% and the 10-year at 4.40%, with policy-sensitive maturities retaining more of the day’s gains than the long bond. A four-basis-point decline in the 10-year real yield coexisted with a three-basis-point rise in breakeven inflation. The result was a split fixed-income message: investors reduced the real-rate penalty after inflation proved less severe than feared, yet restored some compensation for energy risk as oil reversed higher.

The domestic release block supported relief without validating an outright dovish turn. Headline PCE inflation undershot expectations and core inflation matched them, prompting a small reduction in expected Fed tightening. Resilient activity details pointed the other way. New unemployment claims fell, first-quarter growth was revised higher, durable-goods demand outside transportation advanced, and a regional factory survey combined faster activity with the strongest price indexes since 2022. Fed officials consequently retained a restrictive message even as the front end rallied.

Energy changed the intraday shape of the move. Crude initially traded near prewar levels as traffic through the Strait of Hormuz recovered, then climbed after a cargo ship was struck and several vessels turned back. Nominal Treasury yields rebounded from their lows, the long bond finished unchanged, and breakevens rose. Oil therefore ceased to be the clean disinflationary ally it had been on June 24, although its large weekly and monthly decline still limited the scale of the inflation repricing.

Two counterforces kept the rally orderly and conditional. A well-received seven-year Treasury auction and solid bill participation showed that government supply could clear without a broad funding disruption. Abroad, weak demand at Japan’s 20-year auction, an expansive multi-year investment program and a still-hawkish BOJ raised the global clearing rate for long duration. Credit access also remained open at the aggregate level, but SpaceX bonds weakened after their jumbo debut and a new derivatives market priced meaningful protection costs. The close continued duration relief while reopening energy, foreign-supply and issuer-selection risks.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

Treasury gains diminished with maturity. The two- and five-year yields each fell two basis points, to 4.09% and 4.15%, while the 10-year declined one basis point to 4.40% and the 30-year held at 4.86%. The front-end move followed a sharp reversal from Monday’s post-FOMC highs, yet the longer comparison still favored duration: over one month, yields were four basis points lower at two years, 12 lower at five years, 16 lower at 10 years and 21 lower at 30 years. Thursday preserved that broader rally without extending it evenly.

Curve slopes captured the shift in leadership. The 2s10s spread widened one basis point to 31, and 2s30s widened two to 77. The 2s5s10s butterfly fell one basis point to minus 19 as the five-year stayed rich against its wings, while the 2s10s30s butterfly was unchanged at minus 15. Wednesday’s rally had concentrated in the belly and long end; Thursday transferred the incremental gain toward policy-sensitive maturities. Long-run fiscal and inflation compensation consequently remained more resistant than the expected path of near-term policy.

Inflation-linked markets supplied the clearest decomposition. The five-year real yield fell six basis points to 1.92%, the seven-year dropped four to 2.05%, and the 10-year declined four to 2.19%. Twenty- and 30-year real yields each eased two basis points, to 2.51% and 2.69%. At 10 years, the nominal yield fell only one basis point because breakeven inflation rose three to 2.21%. Investors accepted less real compensation across the curve, but the renewed energy tail kept the nominal long end from matching that move.

May PCE inflation initiated the real-yield rally. The headline index rose 0.4% on the month, below the 0.5% median estimate, while core prices increased 0.3% as expected. Annual headline inflation accelerated to 4.1% and core inflation to 3.4%, the latter its highest since 2023, so the release offered relief from an upside surprise rather than evidence that price pressure had normalized. Swaps reduced tightening priced through December to about 34 basis points from roughly 36 at Wednesday’s close, and the implied chance of a July increase fell to about one in three.

The rest of the data kept the reduction in Fed urgency narrow. Initial claims dropped to 215,000 from a revised 227,000, even as insured unemployment rose to 1.821 million from 1.800 million. May durable-goods orders fell 4.5% after an 8.5% April increase, driven by a 14.0% decline in transportation equipment; orders excluding transportation rose 1.3%. First-quarter growth was revised to a 2.1% annualized pace. The Kansas City Fed composite also rose to 11 from 8, with production, orders and employment improving and both principal price indexes reaching their highest readings since 2022.

Fed communication fit that mixed growth-and-inflation configuration. Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee regarded parts of the inflation release as encouraging, but emphasized that core inflation remained too high and was moving in the wrong direction. New York Fed President John Williams called inflation unquestionably elevated and described the current policy stance as well positioned to return it to target. Nine of 19 policymakers had projected at least one increase this year after the prior week’s meeting. Those messages allowed a modest front-end rally after the feared inflation overshoot failed to appear, while preserving a high real-rate floor.

Intraday trading showed how quickly the energy channel could offset the data impulse. Yields across maturities reached their lowest levels in at least a week after the PCE release, and the 30-year touched 4.82%, its lowest since March. Crude’s reversal after the Hormuz incident then pulled nominal yields away from those lows. Only shorter maturities remained clearly richer late in the session. The close therefore separated lower expected policy urgency from renewed concern that energy and shipping disruption could slow progress on headline inflation.

Coupon supply reinforced the orderly side of the rates story. Treasury’s $44 billion seven-year auction was awarded at 4.260%, matching the level indicated in trading immediately before the 1 p.m. ET deadline. Demand was described as good after the indicated yield had traded as high as 4.40% following the auction announcement. The result did not create a new concession signal, but it showed that the belly could absorb the final fixed-rate sale before the July 7 three-year auction even after two days of falling yields.

Short-bill auctions also cleared in size. The $70 billion 28-day sale stopped at a 3.610% high rate with a 2.74 bid-to-cover ratio; indirect bidders took 65.14% of accepted competitive tenders, dealers 31.23% and direct bidders 3.63%. The $75 billion 56-day sale stopped at 3.660% with 2.79 coverage, a 69.52% indirect share, a 27.85% dealer share and a 2.62% direct share. Both securities issue and settle on June 30, keeping Thursday’s bidding outcome separate from the later cash transfer.

Cash yields were differentiated around those sales. The four-week coupon-equivalent yield rose six basis points to 3.68% and the eight-week added two to 3.74%, while six weeks was unchanged at 3.69%. Thirteen- and 17-week yields each fell one basis point, and the 26-week yield held at 3.94%. The local pressure around the auctioned maturities did not spread into a common front-end funding move. Taken with the seven-year result, the pattern leaves the rates close as a real-yield rally constrained by energy inflation, resilient demand and a still-restrictive Fed.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Broad listed credit returns were slightly positive. LQD gained 0.0823%, HYG rose 0.0376% and JNK added 0.0936%. The high-grade fund received only a small duration benefit because the Treasury rally faded into the close, while the two speculative-grade funds held near flat. This was a stable aggregate tape, neither a broad search for credit risk nor a withdrawal of financing. Issuer-level pricing carried much more information than the modest moves in the listed funds.

SpaceX supplied the most important current cash-credit update. After raising $25 billion in its debut high-grade sale on Tuesday, its bonds weakened relative to Treasuries. The 10-year notes traded at a spread of 1.57 percentage points on Thursday after pricing at 1.40 percentage points. The widening changed the interpretation of the completed deal: exceptional primary capacity remained evident, but secondary buyers required more compensation once the bonds entered normal trading and the peak orderbook was no longer supporting the transaction.

A new derivatives market made that discrimination easier to express. Dealers began actively making markets in five-year SpaceX credit-default swaps, with one indication showing a midpoint near 1.255 percentage points annually, or about $125,500 per year for $10 million of protection. Comparable protection on similarly rated Intel debt was around 0.64 percentage point. The comparison does not negate SpaceX’s high-grade ratings or large liquidity position; it shows that investors assign a distinct premium to its capital intensity, governance concentration and long path toward free cash generation.

Japanese regional banks demonstrated a different funding transmission from higher policy rates. Yokohama Financial Group sold its inaugural ¥12 billion three-year senior bond at 27 basis points over JGBs. Final orders reached about 1.2 times the issue, with life insurers, investment advisers and trust banks among the buyers. The BOJ’s increase to a 1% policy rate intensified competition for deposits, encouraging regional lenders with continuing loan demand to supplement deposits with wholesale funding. Access was available, though the modest book left pricing and future issuance sensitive to local funding needs.

Credit differentiation was also becoming more explicit in China’s domestic market. Regulators asked rating firms to review the concentration of top-tier grades and introduced issuance spreads over comparable government bonds as one assessment metric. An issuer whose spread exceeds 200 basis points could risk losing AAA status, subject to other factors. At the end of the first quarter, 27% of more than 6,000 issuers were rated AAA and another 32% AA+. A less compressed ratings scale could improve risk separation, with higher borrowing costs concentrated among issuers that previously benefited from the top domestic label.

Strategy’s preferred shares showed how funding can deteriorate outside conventional bonds even when broad credit remains calm. STRC had fallen to about $75 from its $100 issue price as Bitcoin traded below $60,000 and buyers retreated. The perpetual security has no claim on Strategy’s Bitcoin holdings, no maturity date, and dividends that the board may reduce or suspend; the company carries a B- rating. Its rising yield reflects strain in a funding vehicle designed to support continued Bitcoin accumulation and highlights the weaker protection available lower in the capital structure.

The combined message was open access with more visible post-pricing consequences. Treasury supply cleared, listed credit returns were steady and a regional Japanese bank reached a new investor base. SpaceX’s cash spread and CDS market nevertheless showed that a successful jumbo sale can leave investors managing meaningful residual risk, while Strategy’s preferred exposed the fragility of funding dependent on an underlying volatile asset. Aggregate volume remained strong; secondary pricing, structure and creditor seniority determined where the strain appeared.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Japan presented the strongest foreign long-duration counterweight. The 20-year JGB auction’s bid-to-cover ratio fell to 2.97, its weakest since May 2025 and below the 12-month average. The tail was the widest since January, and the lowest accepted price fell well below the pre-sale estimate. Banks appeared to provide much of the demand as insurers and global managers reduced exposure to super-long JGBs. The weak result raised the prospect of renewed curve steepening just as U.S. real yields were falling.

Fiscal policy amplified the auction signal. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s strategy seeks more than ¥370 trillion, or $2.3 trillion, of public and private investment over 14 years in areas including artificial intelligence and semiconductors. Government projections assume annual fiscal spending around ¥10 trillion and contemplate bridging bonds, with tax revenue and spending savings also part of the funding mix. Growth benefits would arrive gradually, whereas issuance and a temporary increase in the debt ratio could appear earlier. That sequencing demands more term premium from long JGBs.

Monetary policy offered little immediate relief to the buyer base. BOJ board member Naoki Tamura described quarter-point increases every few months toward a neutral rate near 2% as his baseline, after the bank raised the policy rate to 1% the prior week. Governor Kazuo Ueda reiterated that further moves depend on the economy and inflation. Persistent yen weakness adds imported-price pressure. Higher local policy and sovereign yields increase the hurdle Japanese institutions apply when comparing JGBs with currency-hedged Treasuries, helping explain the U.S. long bond’s resistance to Thursday’s real-yield rally.

Europe’s duration signal was softer because energy had already retraced much of its wartime rise. Oxford Economics dropped its forecast for another ECB increase after the June move, and other forecasters reduced their expected number of steps. Markets still priced one quarter-point increase this year, while ECB Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel and Governing Council member Emmanuel Moulin retained inflation vigilance. Moulin described the recent 25-basis-point increase as moderate and noted that oil had fallen below the assumptions used in the latest forecasts. The policy debate shifted toward a possible hawkish pause, not renewed accommodation.

Gilts illustrated the duration benefit from that energy relief. The 10-year UK yield had fallen to around 4.7%, its lowest since mid-March, and broad gilt returns reached a three-month high even through domestic political uncertainty. Lower crude reduced the imported-inflation burden and allowed global bond direction to dominate. Thursday’s late oil rebound qualified this support without erasing it, leaving European duration more favorable than Japan’s supply-heavy long end but still exposed to another round of energy pass-through.

China moved in the opposite direction through money-market design. The PBOC introduced an overnight reverse-repo instrument alongside its 1.4% seven-day benchmark, which has been unchanged since May 2025. The new tool gives officials a more direct way to steer overnight funding and narrow volatility in money-market rates. Traders associated the framework change with an easing bias amid weaker second-quarter momentum. More precise short-term liquidity control supports China’s domestic duration market, contrasting with the higher clearing rate created by Japan’s policy normalization and fiscal supply.

Tokyo inflation then supplied a later Asia-window update. Released at 8:30 a.m. JST on June 26, after the New York close, the capital’s CPI excluding fresh food rose 1.6% from a year earlier and the measure excluding fresh food and energy increased 1.9%. The key gauge accelerated for the first time in eight months, led by water-service fees after subsidies ended. Its timing makes it an input for the next JGB session; Thursday’s U.S. long-end resistance was already visible through the weak auction, fiscal plan and yen-linked inflation risk.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

Broad emerging-market debt was almost unchanged despite a softer dollar on the day. EMB slipped 0.0104%, while the Dollar Index fell 0.1771% to 101.43. The currency gauge was still 0.5751% higher over one week and 2.1244% higher over one month, preserving a restrictive external backdrop. Stable aggregate performance concealed a large redistribution between commodity exporters, highly rated sovereign issuers and countries negotiating debt relief.

African oil exporters remained exposed to the broader retreat in crude even after Thursday’s rebound. Republic of Congo eurobonds had lost more than 2% during June, and Angola, Cameroon, Gabon and Nigeria more than 1%, against an average 0.5% gain across emerging markets. Angola’s 2048 dollar-bond yield rose more than 60 basis points from its early-June low to 9.94%; Congo’s 2036 yield climbed about 66 basis points to 10.01%, and Nigeria’s 2034 yield rose 33 basis points to 7.33%. Cheaper oil removed export-income support and forced renewed attention to fiscal execution and external funding.

The country differences within that group were material. Weak Chinese demand pushed some Angolan crude grades to their lowest prices in more than a decade and reduced buying interest in Congolese oil. Nigerian grades held up better because the Dangote refinery supplied domestic demand, while prior currency-market reforms improved the country’s resilience. Thursday’s Hormuz incident restored an immediate security premium to crude, but WTI and Brent remained down more than 25% and 27% over one month. Oil credits therefore gained short-run price support without recovering the earlier terms-of-trade advantage.

China demonstrated hard-currency access at the other end of the sovereign spectrum. The Ministry of Finance marketed as much as €5 billion of five-, eight- and 12-year euro bonds at initial guidance of 15, 22 and 33 basis points over mid-swaps. The transaction could become China’s largest euro sovereign sale and came only seven months after a €4 billion issue drew more than €100 billion of demand. The early return to the market sought to capture favorable funding before possible European inflation pressure and further ECB tightening; the stated terms established marketing, not completed pricing.

Debt-relief negotiations exposed a different external-financing risk. China called for legal and technical measures to limit aggressive bondholder litigation under the Group of 20 Common Framework. In Ethiopia, investors had begun the process of suing over $1 billion of defaulted notes after failing to reach terms acceptable to both the bondholder group and official creditors co-led by China. The dispute threatens the coordination needed to translate official-sector relief into a durable private-creditor settlement and leaves preferred-creditor status, burden sharing and enforcement central to recovery values.

Local-currency sovereigns showed that policy credibility can offset part of the global dollar constraint. Hungary’s 10-year benchmark yield fell to 5.16%, within half a percentage point of comparable UK debt, after the premium over gilts had stood at 2.6 percentage points in March. Its spread over Germany narrowed to 2.3 percentage points, about half the level three months earlier. Access to more than €16 billion of previously frozen EU aid and a pledge to meet euro-entry criteria by 2030 supported convergence despite Hungary’s lower credit rating.

The EM tape was consequently stable only in aggregate. China could bring record-scale euro supply at tight initial guidance, Hungary benefited from an institutional-convergence story, and African exporters lost the oil premium that had supported their dollar bonds. Ethiopia faced a legal coordination problem that market access alone could not solve. Dollar weakness on the day eased immediate pressure, yet the currency’s monthly strength and high U.S. real yields kept refinancing conditions sensitive to commodity revenue, policy execution and creditor structure.

## Cross asset

Equity performance argued against a broad growth scare as the source of the Treasury rally. The S&P 500 was virtually unchanged, down 0.0099%, while the Nasdaq 100 gained 0.7538% and the semiconductor index rose 3.5864%. Micron’s outlook revived confidence in AI hardware, although Apple fell 6.1% after raising prices across Macs, iPads and home devices. The VIX increased 1.3956% to 18.89. Strong chip leadership alongside higher volatility described concentrated dispersion, not a uniform flight from risk.

Oil aligned directly with the rise in breakeven inflation. WTI gained 2.2462% to $71.92 and Brent rose 2.0613% to $75.26 after the vessel strike disrupted the earlier normalization in Hormuz traffic. Both had traded near prewar levels before the reversal. Their one-week losses remained about 6%, and their one-month declines exceeded 25%, so the daily move restored a geopolitical premium without re-establishing the full wartime inflation shock. That time-profile explains why breakevens rose while real yields and short Treasury yields still declined.

The dollar and gold offered a mild hedge signal. The Dollar Index eased to 101.43 after a strong monthly advance, and gold gained 1.0074% to $4,030.50. Bullion was still 4.5832% lower over one week and 10.8494% lower over one month. A softer dollar and firmer gold matched renewed geopolitical concern on Thursday, while their longer moves remained consistent with elevated real rates and a relatively hawkish Fed. Neither market signaled a break in dollar liquidity.

Cross assets therefore complicated the clean disinflation narrative from June 24 without replacing it with recession risk. Semiconductors rallied, the broad equity index held flat and credit funds were stable, supporting the resilient-demand side of the macro releases. Oil and gold reacted to renewed Hormuz uncertainty, lifting the inflation tail and restraining the long bond. The coherent interpretation is a controlled real-yield rally in which policy urgency eased slightly, while energy security and concentrated technology risk prevented a stronger nominal-duration move.

## Forward Market Significance

At 10:00 a.m. ET on June 26, the final University of Michigan consumer survey and the New York Fed’s Multivariate Core Trend Inflation measure provide the nearest test of whether Thursday’s PCE relief can survive a renewed energy premium. Michigan’s preliminary June readings were 48.9 for sentiment, 4.6% for one-year inflation expectations and 3.4% for long-run expectations. Lower final expectations and a softer trend-inflation measure, especially if crude remains near its post-incident range, would support the decline in real yields and confirm that the PCE result reduced policy urgency without unanchoring longer inflation pricing. Firm or rising expectations alongside persistent core-trend pressure would challenge that split, transmitting through breakevens, two- to 10-year real yields and December Fed pricing, and would reopen the possibility that Thursday’s bull steepening was only temporary relief from a feared upside surprise.

Japan’s buyer-base question moves through three sovereign auctions: about ¥4.1 trillion of three-month bills around 12:35 p.m. JST on June 26, roughly ¥2.8 trillion of two-year JGBs on June 30 and about ¥2.6 trillion of new 10-year bonds on July 2. Broad demand and stable yields across the front end and benchmark maturity would indicate that the weak 20-year sale was concentrated in super-long duration, resolving part of the concern that fiscal expansion and BOJ normalization are lifting the entire domestic clearing curve. Repeatedly soft demand or renewed steepening, particularly if June 30 activity releases and the July 1 Tankan show firm investment and price expectations, would confirm a higher Japanese term and policy premium. The sensitive transmission runs through the yen, JGB slopes, Japanese demand for currency-hedged foreign bonds and the U.S. 30-year yield.

U.S. quarter-end cash demand faces a dense sequence before the July 2 early close. Treasury sells $92 billion of 13-week and $79 billion of 26-week bills on June 29 for July 2 settlement, followed by an $80 billion six-week sale on June 30; June payrolls and weekly claims arrive at 8:30 a.m. ET on July 2, paired four- and eight-week auctions follow at 11:30, and fixed-income trading is scheduled to close at 2:00 p.m. Orderly coverage, broad end-buyer participation and stable cash-bill yields would confirm that Thursday’s auctions reflected durable liquidity through the settlement turn. A cooler labor report would extend that signal into Fed pricing and the belly. Dealer-heavy absorption, abrupt bill cheapening or strong payroll and wage growth would challenge the orderly bull-steepening thesis by combining balance-sheet pressure with renewed tightening risk in a shortened session.

section_summaries.market_read: The close favors near-term policy relief but leaves long-duration conviction constrained.
section_summaries.next_check: Oil’s persistence and the next inflation signals must show whether relief can extend.
section_summaries.summary: Policy-sensitive Treasuries led a modest bull steepening after PCE inflation avoided an upside surprise, while falling real yields carried most of the rally. Oil’s rebound restored some inflation compensation, and resilient activity, restrictive Fed guidance, foreign supply pressure and selective credit weakness kept the move conditional.

section_summaries.market_read: The rally reflects lower policy urgency, not a break in the restrictive-rate regime.
section_summaries.next_check: Inflation expectations, core trends and Fed pricing will test whether bull steepening persists.
section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: Front-end yields fell as PCE inflation came in no worse than expected, but robust labor, demand and factory-price signals limited the easing in Fed pricing. Real yields declined more than nominals, breakevens rose, and solid coupon and bill auctions showed supply cleared without a broad funding strain.

section_summaries.market_read: Stable aggregate credit masks widening gaps in structure, seniority and issuer quality.
section_summaries.next_check: Post-deal SpaceX pricing and lower-tier funding stress remain the clearest tests.
section_summaries.summary: Aggregate credit remained steady and funding access stayed open, yet secondary markets demanded sharper issuer discrimination. SpaceX weakened after its jumbo debut, Japanese regional-bank access came with a modest book, and Strategy’s preferred shares exposed structural fragility where volatile collateral and weak creditor protections underpin financing.

section_summaries.market_read: Japanese term premium is the main foreign constraint on the U.S. long-duration rally.
section_summaries.next_check: Upcoming JGB auctions must separate super-long weakness from a broader buyer retreat.
section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Japan’s weak long-bond auction, fiscal investment plan and ongoing BOJ normalization raised the clearing rate for global duration, limiting U.S. long-end performance. Europe benefited from energy’s broader retracement, while China’s new overnight liquidity tool pointed toward more precise easing and a contrasting domestic duration backdrop.

section_summaries.market_read: Dollar relief eased immediate pressure, but commodity revenue and creditor structure still rule.
section_summaries.next_check: Oil-export revenues, China’s euro pricing and Ethiopia’s negotiations remain decisive.
section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: Emerging-market debt was stable in aggregate, but the benign surface concealed divergent funding paths. African oil exporters faced weaker terms of trade, China retained tight euro-market access, Hungary benefited from institutional convergence, and Ethiopia’s creditor dispute showed how legal coordination can dominate recovery prospects.

section_summaries.market_read: Risk resilience validates lower real yields while oil keeps the inflation tail alive.
section_summaries.next_check: The durability of chip leadership and the Hormuz premium will test that balance.
section_summaries.summary: Cross assets rejected a broad recession interpretation: semiconductor strength, a flat broad equity market and stable credit supported resilient demand. Oil and gold reflected renewed geopolitical concern, while the dollar softened only modestly, leaving the Treasury move as controlled real-yield relief rather than a liquidity break.

section_summaries.market_read: The rally survives only if inflation, global duration and cash demand remain orderly.
section_summaries.next_check: The June 26-to-July 2 sequence will reveal which constraint becomes dominant.
section_summaries.summary: Three tests determine whether the conditional rally can persist: near-term U.S. inflation expectations, Japan’s auction demand across maturities, and quarter-end U.S. bill absorption alongside labor data. Benign outcomes would extend real-yield relief; firmer inflation, weak Japanese demand or funding pressure would restore term and policy premia.

methodology_note: Market levels are shown on an NY-close basis unless otherwise noted.

bullets: 10Y TIPS real yield 2.19% (-4 bp) | 10Y breakeven inflation 2.21% (+3 bp)
UST 2s10s curve 31 bp (+1 bp) | UST 2s30s curve 77 bp (+2 bp)
overall_summary.headline: Treasuries rallied as softer inflation cut real yields, while oil restrained duration
overall_summary.session_narrative: At the NY close, the two- and five-year sectors retained modest gains while the long bond finished unchanged, producing a small bull steepening. A softer-than-feared PCE print lowered real yields and marginally reduced expected Fed tightening. Oil’s rebound lifted breakevens and, alongside firm activity, hawkish Fed guidance and weak Japanese duration demand, kept the nominal long end from matching the real-rate rally.
overall_summary.summary: The NY close preserved a Treasury rally led by policy-sensitive maturities as PCE inflation avoided an upside surprise. Lower real yields carried the move, but firmer oil, resilient activity and restrictive Fed guidance limited nominal long-end gains.

watch_events.market_implication: Benign readings favor lower real yields; firmness would lift breakevens and Fed pricing.
watch_events.market_trigger: Lower expectations and a softer trend measure would preserve Thursday’s PCE relief.
watch_events.title: U.S. Inflation-Expectation Test
watch_events.watch_line: Track final Michigan expectations and the core-trend measure against renewed oil pressure.

watch_events.market_implication: Wider strain would lift Japanese term premium and pressure yen-hedged U.S. long-duration demand.
watch_events.market_trigger: Broad demand would isolate weakness to super-long bonds; repeated softness would signal wider strain.
watch_events.title: Japan Sovereign Auction Demand
watch_events.watch_line: Compare demand and yield stability across bills, two-year and new 10-year JGB supply.

watch_events.market_implication: Stress would lift front-end funding and tightening premia, undermining the bull-steepening close.
watch_events.market_trigger: Dealer-heavy absorption, abrupt bill cheapening or firm wages would challenge orderly liquidity.
watch_events.title: U.S. Quarter-End Cash and Labor Test
watch_events.watch_line: Track bill coverage and end-buyer demand into settlement alongside payrolls and claims.
